title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] FRANCESCO ROMANO: Hey everyone! Do you remember when
developing Android apps meant designing for a
single screen, just one app, one screen, touch
as the only input type? We've been interacting
with our digital world one window at a time. But now look around. We have phones that
unfold into tablets. Tablets that dock with keyboards
and lights like laptops and even support for
external displays. We're using styluses for
precision, keyboards for speed, and trackpads for
nuanced control. The interaction model has
grown far beyond a single touch on a small screen. Today, users are expecting
a desktop level experience from their Android
devices, especially on this larger form factors. Users aren't just consuming
content on these larger screens. They want to create, to
work, to be productive. And they expect desktop
level interactions. This is why Android is
investing in desktop windowing. This feature has been
released as a developer preview in October 2024,
and I'm happy to share that it will be included in
the stable Android 16 AOSP. Release. We've been working
closely with OEMs to create a consistent
experience that's fully integrated into
the core Android UI. Desktop windowing is more than
just allowing multiple apps on screen at the same time. It comes with a new system UI
to make users more productive and a new set of APIs
to allow developers to build [INAUDIBLE]
experiences that leverage this powerful feature. And that's what we're
here to talk about today. So let's explore how to make
your apps ready for this new era of Android interactions. The first pillar for
a great experience is providing an adaptive UI. This is not just supporting
landscape mode anymore. Your app can live in a window
of almost any size and aspect ratio, chosen by the user. Even if the tablet
has a bigger display, that doesn't mean that the app
will live in a bigger window. In desktop windowing,
the minimum window size is 386 per 352 DP, which is
smaller than most phones.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
ratio, chosen by the user. Even if the tablet
has a bigger display, that doesn't mean that the app
will live in a bigger window. In desktop windowing,
the minimum window size is 386 per 352 DP, which is
smaller than most phones. And this fundamentally changes
how we need to think about UI. Because users are not just
making small adjustments to the window, they can expand
your app to fill a huge Canvas. The question becomes,
does your UI just stretch or does it adapt? Does it reflow intelligently,
maybe revealing more panels? Can you offer more powerful
features, richer information display? How do you implement
something like this? The foundation remains the same. Use the window size classes as
inputs to your layout decisions. They give you essential context
about the available space. Don't reinvent the wheel and
leverage the supporting Jetpack libraries for test and
optimize components. Get inspired by the
canonical layouts to build the perfect
experience for your use case. We have a bunch of
updates for developers to support the implementation
of adaptive UI. For more information,
please check out the link in the description. But a great desktop
experience is more than just looking good at any size. It's about workflow
and productivity. A key enabler here is to allow
multiple instances of your app. Let's say your app
can edit documents. You really want users to be
able to open two documents at the same time on
different windows, so they can reference one
while editing the others. Or maybe you want to share
content between the documents with drag and drop. There are essentially three ways
to start an additional instance of your app, from a UI
affordance of your own app, from the system UI, and
this is new to desktop windowing with the
drag and drop gesture. Let's see how to
implement all of them. In order to support
multi-instance, first of all, you need to define a launch
mode for your activity that supports multiple instances. In this case, I pick
single instance per task.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
Let's see how to
implement all of them. In order to support
multi-instance, first of all, you need to define a launch
mode for your activity that supports multiple instances. In this case, I pick
single instance per task. This activity can
only run as the root activity of the task, the first
activity that created the task. And therefore, there
can only be one instance of this activity per task. Remember that a new task
in desktop windowing will always result
in a new window. You can now start multiple
instances of your activity by adding those
flags in the intent. When using the new
task flag alone, if a task is already
running for the activity you are now starting, then a new
activity will not be started. Instead, the current
task will simply be brought to the
front of the screen with the state it was last in. If the flag is paired with
flag activity multiple task, then it will skip the
search for a matching task and unconditionally
start a new task, which is required if you want to
always open a separate window. The exact user experience
depends on your custom UI. For example, Chrome
provides the entry point from its contextual layer. Next, system UI. You can add this property in
your manifest to tell the system launcher that your activity
supports multiple instances. So users may be able to start
another instance directly from the launcher,
from the taskbar, or from other system
UI components. One example is the
new Window menu that pops up from the top
app handle, which is shown only if that property is set. Then it gives users a hint that
app supports multi-instance. This is already great, but there
is an even more natural way to start another instance. Look at how Chrome starts a new
instance by dragging a tab out of the app window. We can implement that
by leveraging the drag and drop API from the
Compose Foundation library. The important parts
are the two flags that customize drag
and drop behavior. DRAG FLAG GLOBAL
SAME APPLICATION indicates that
the drag operation
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
We can implement that
by leveraging the drag and drop API from the
Compose Foundation library. The important parts
are the two flags that customize drag
and drop behavior. DRAG FLAG GLOBAL
SAME APPLICATION indicates that
the drag operation can cross window boundaries
for multiple instances of the same application. DRAG FLAG START INTENT
SENDER ON UNHANDLED DRAG tells the system that if the
drop event happens outside of any visible window,
it should be delegated to the system with solve. You may be asking, OK, but
how does the system know which activity to start? Well, we are providing the
information in this function. Let's have a deeper look. The clip data needs
to contain the intent sender of the activity
that you want to launch. When the drop event ends
outside of any visible window, the system will capture this
intent and start the activity. Now I want to check if you
were paying close attention and noticed something
interesting about the Chrome UI. Yes, Chrome is using a
custom app header bar. We already saw how all the
apps in desktop windowing have this header, which is
reported as a caption bar type of insets. It's important to account for
this when drawing content edge to edge, but you can do more. In particular, Chrome is
optimizing the use of space by drawing the tabs
directly in the header bar. Apps can use this
new appearance type to make the header bar
transparent to allow custom content inside the header bar. If an app uses this API,
the system automatically hides the app's
name from the bar to leave more space
for the custom content. Let's see an example. Look how we are rendering the
content only if the capture bar is visible, and
how we draw a row that has the same dimensions
of the header bar. Then we can add
our tabs within it, but we need to avoid overlapping
the system components, like the app logo and
the window controls. Calculating the right
padding requires measuring the caption bar and
subtracting the areas reserved for system components.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
but we need to avoid overlapping
the system components, like the app logo and
the window controls. Calculating the right
padding requires measuring the caption bar and
subtracting the areas reserved for system components. You can learn more about it at
the link in the description. Desktop windowing
works great on tablets. And I'm happy to
share that Android is working to push it further,
with external displays. Coming to the beta releases of
25 Q3, as a developer preview, the desktop windowing
experience will be enabled on compatible
devices when connected to an external display. When the phone is connected
to an external display, the phone state
remains the same, and the new desktop session
starts on the external display. The phone and the external
display operate independently. And apps are specific
to the display on which they are running. When a desktop windowing-enabled
device, like a tablet is connected to an
external monitor, the desktop session is
extended across both displays. They then function as
one continuous system, allowing windows, content,
and cursor to move freely between the two displays. Let's talk about
some best practices to ensure a smooth and
high-quality user experience. First, do not assume the display
object will remain constant. When a user connects or
disconnects an external monitor or even moves your app
window between displays, the underlying display object
associated with your apps, context, or window can change. Its properties like size,
resolution, refresh rate, and density can
all be different. Hardcoding values based
on the phone screen, for example, will break
layouts on an external display. Second, you must account for
density configuration changes. External monitors can
have vastly different pixel densities. So make sure your app
responds correctly to these density changes. This means using
density-independent pixels for layout, providing
density-specific resources, and ensuring your UI
scales appropriately. Finally, think beyond
just the screen.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
So make sure your app
responds correctly to these density changes. This means using
density-independent pixels for layout, providing
density-specific resources, and ensuring your UI
scales appropriately. Finally, think beyond
just the screen. When users connect to
an external monitor, they often create a more
desktop-like environment. This frequently involves using
external keyboards, mice, trackpads, webcams,
microphones, and speakers. So ensure your app
works seamlessly with these peripherals. Handle keyboard shortcuts,
mouse pointer interactions, correctly support external
cameras or microphones, and respect audio
output routing. So what's the next step? The great news is that enhanced
support for external monitors, including many of the
capabilities we have discussed today, is coming soon as a
developer preview in the Android beta program. Stay tuned to learn how to
try the connected display experience. This is the perfect
opportunity to identify areas for improvement and
start implementing these best practices now, ensuring your app
is ready to deliver a fantastic experience as more users
adopt multi-display setups. Remember that building apps that
work great on different devices means more than just
fitting to the screen. It's also about using
powerful interaction tools. Join Miguel to learn how
improved stylus support can boost productivity and
creativity in your apps. MIGUEL MONTEMAYOR:
Thanks, Francesco. Styluses are transforming
Android devices into powerful tools for both
productivity and creativity. Users expect inking to
feel as natural and fluid as writing on paper. To create a simple, fluid
inking experience like this, it generally required
a lot of investment in graphics and geometry
just to get started. Developers had to
handle everything from generating stroke
shapes with stylus input, to rendering those strokes
quickly and efficiently, and even performing complex
geometric calculations for things like
erasing and selection. We built the ink Jetpack
library to simplify
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
from generating stroke
shapes with stylus input, to rendering those strokes
quickly and efficiently, and even performing complex
geometric calculations for things like
erasing and selection. We built the ink Jetpack
library to simplify the process of creating,
rendering, and manipulating beautiful ink strokes. It provides a powerful
and modular toolkit for integrating rich inking
features into your app. The library consists
of six modules. The Strokes module is the
foundation of the ink library, handling core data types for
interacting with ink input. The Authoring module
manages the capture of user input and real
time rendering of strokes and facilitates
low-latency rendering. The Brush module manages
the visual style of strokes through properties like
color, size, and texture. The Geometry module provides
geometric primitives for shape manipulation
and enables features like stroke erasing
and selection. The Rendering
module incorporates canvasStrokeRenderer and
viewStrokeRenderer, offering performant rendering pipelines
with anti-aliasing support for Compose and
view-based UIs. Finally, the Storage module
provides efficient storage of stroke input data. As a developer, you can
use only the modules needed for your app's architecture. We've received great feedback
from our early partners. Ink API significantly reduced
the workload required for them to implement responsive
fluid inking in their app. Now let's take a look
at the Brush module and some of the
recent improvements we made with custom brushes. You can use the Brush module
in its factory methods to get started quickly
using stock brushes for pressure-sensitive
pins, highlighters, markers, and dashed lines. Here you see how I quickly
created a brush with the stock pressure pin and set the color,
size, and epsilon of the brush. We now have added support
for customized brushes with new experimental
APIs, which lets you unleash your
creativity to implement fun, expressive brushes,
or just match your existing set of brushes.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
pressure pin and set the color,
size, and epsilon of the brush. We now have added support
for customized brushes with new experimental
APIs, which lets you unleash your
creativity to implement fun, expressive brushes,
or just match your existing set of brushes. Here are just a few examples of
the many types of custom brushes supported in the Ink API. You can customize many
attributes like brush tip shape, layering multiple tips,
and using particles to create these effects. To use a custom
brush in your app, you'll need to do two things. First, load the
brush family file that you created or has
been distributed separately in your app. If the brush
doesn't use textures like pens, highlighters,
and markers, you can load the brush file
with the brush family decoder throw method, as shown here. Then you can use that brush
family when creating the brush. When saving an
inking file, your app must save all the brush
families used by any stroke. If the brush uses textures like
a pencil or charcoal texture, you'll need to make
sure to correctly load, manage, and render ink
strokes with the texture. This code demonstrates
initializing a texture bitmap store, loading brush families
with texture decoding, and then later will set
up inProgressStrokesView and canvasStrokeRenderer
to use the texture. As a first step,
interactivity, we initialize a texture bitmap
store named textureStore. Step 2, using serialized
version of the brushes we created earlier, we
load brush family data from raw resources for
sparkle brush and pencil brush using the decoder throw method. Lastly, step 3,
our renderers need to know where to
get our textures. So we pass the texture store
to both inProgressStrokesView and the canvasStrokeRenderer. Additionally, we're working on
Native Compose APIs coming soon that make it even
easier to integrate Ink API in Compose-based apps. The last update on Ink
API I want to share is improvements we've made
to the new Storage module. Ink API has recently introduced
serialization of stroke input
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – MmeJSLAnB-M
text:
that make it even
easier to integrate Ink API in Compose-based apps. The last update on Ink
API I want to share is improvements we've made
to the new Storage module. Ink API has recently introduced
serialization of stroke input batch, which uses
delta compression algorithms to significantly
reduce the storage size of stroke input data. Along with Ink API, we're
excited to share that more OEM devices now support Notes Roll. Notes Roll is a system
level default app roll that makes
it easy for users to access their preferred
note taking app. Your app can be opened
in a floating window or from the lock
screen for quick access and easy multitasking. Select devices with Android
15 now support Notes Roll. To learn more about how your
app can support and register for Notes Roll and to get
started building with Ink API today, check out the links in
the description of the video. Now that you've seen all
the ways Android is letting developers and users do more
than ever with their Android devices, like investing in
desktop windowing to provide a desktop level user experience,
and improving the ink API to reduce the
effort needed to build natural and fluid inking
experiences in your app, now it's up to you to
build experiences that let your users do more
in your app and on Android. Check out the resources
linked below to leverage the latest APIs to build
desktop quality experiences and fully utilize
other modes of input, like keyboard,
mouse, and stylus. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
